narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 97% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for law school admission was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to flag risks like this to help schools maintain high bar passage rates. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings from earlier years, especially your position in the lowest decile for Year 3 grades at your school, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 25 was the next biggest influence in the same direction, signaling to the model a notable gap compared to what typically leads to passing the bar. Even your Year 1 decile ranking in the bottom of your class added to this concern, reinforcing the pattern. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it's above the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset. Your middle-class family income had a minor push toward the failure prediction, while your choice of full-time study offered a small positive nudge. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early academic deciles are much lower than their mid-range averages around 5.5, and your LSAT is well below the 36.7 norm, even though your GPA shines brighter than most. It's tough to hear, especially when you know your strengths, but this model weighs these predictors heavily based on patterns from past bar-takers. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking ways to improve your academic standing through targeted study or additional coursework, and perhaps exploring post-baccalaureate programs to strengthen your profile. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—keep pushing, and consider reapplying after building those credentials. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
